data.title: F.N.B. Corp Earnings Call Highlights Profitable Growth
data.description:
F.N.B. Corp ((FNB)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

F.N.B. Corp’s latest earnings call struck a distinctly upbeat tone, with management highlighting broad-based strength in earnings, balance sheet health and capital returns even as they acknowledged near-term margin and cost headwinds. Leadership framed elevated investment in technology, data and customer-facing initiatives as a deliberate trade-off to support durable growth, efficiency gains and shareholder value over the longer term.

Strong Profitability and EPS Growth

F.N.B. reported net income of $137 million and diluted EPS of $0.38, marking a robust 19% increase from a year earlier. Management emphasized that the bottom-line advance reflects continued profitability and positive operating leverage, underscoring that revenue is growing faster than expenses despite a competitive banking backdrop.

PPNR and Operating Leverage

Pre-provision net revenue rose 17% year-over-year, signaling healthy core earnings power before credit costs. This performance translated into nearly 5% positive operating leverage, suggesting F.N.B. is successfully scaling its franchise and extracting efficiency gains while still funding strategic growth investments.

Loan Growth Acceleration

Total loans and leases ended the quarter at $35.1 billion, representing 3.9% annualized growth versus the prior quarter. Both consumer and commercial lending contributed, with about $198 million of consumer originations and $136 million of commercial loans, and C&I balances showing more than 4% annualized spot growth.

Deposit Stability and Loan-to-Deposit Ratio

Spot total deposits reached $38.9 billion, increasing $142 million sequentially and signaling stable funding in a tight market. Noninterest-bearing deposits grew at a 3.6% annualized pace and remained a healthy 26% of the mix, helping keep the loan-to-deposit ratio steady at about 90% and supporting disciplined balance sheet management.

Capital Strength and Shareholder Returns

Capital levels remain solid, with a CET1 ratio of 11.4% and tangible common equity near 9%, providing meaningful flexibility for growth and capital deployment. Reflecting this strength, the board authorized an additional $250 million in share repurchases, leaving $300 million of capacity, and lifted the quarterly dividend by 8% to $0.13 per share.

Tangible Book Value and ROATCE

Tangible book value per share climbed 11% year-over-year to $12.06, underscoring steady value creation for investors even through a choppy rate cycle. Return on average tangible common equity registered a strong 13.2%, showing that F.N.B. is generating attractive returns on its capital base while still investing for future growth.

Net Interest Income and Margin Momentum

Net interest income increased nearly 11% compared with the prior year, confirming that higher earning assets and disciplined pricing are offsetting modest margin pressure. While the first-quarter net interest margin dipped 3 basis points sequentially to 3.25%, management pointed to a March exit margin around 3.30% and signaled expectations for gradual expansion over the remainder of the year.

Fee Revenue Growth and Business Diversification

Noninterest income rose 3.7% to $91 million, demonstrating the benefits of diversification beyond spread income. Capital markets revenue jumped 27.8% to $6.8 million and wealth management fees edged up 2.8% to $21.8 million, and leadership sees further upside from investment banking, public finance, treasury services and wealth initiatives.

Efficiency and Expense Discipline

The efficiency ratio improved to 56.1% from 58.5% a year ago, indicating that revenue growth is outpacing the rise in operating costs. Management stressed that this improvement came even as the company stepped up spending on technology and growth platforms, reinforcing its narrative of balancing near-term cost discipline with long-term strategic investment.

Asset Quality and Reserves

Credit quality remained resilient, with a funded provision of $19.4 million supporting loan growth and modest charge-offs. Reserves held steady at $443 million, or 1.26% of loans, and rise to about 1.32% when including unamortized discounts, while nonperforming loans are covered nearly four times by reserves, giving F.N.B. a substantial buffer against potential weakness.

Strategic Wins and Innovation Initiatives

Management highlighted several strategic wins, including becoming an exclusive retail and tower services provider for Penn State, which strengthens regional presence and brand visibility. The bank also rolled out innovative services like foreign-currency-capable ATMs at Pittsburgh International Airport and continues to invest in AI, digital platforms and a 360-degree customer-view product, underpinning its customer-first strategy.

NIM Pressure from Fed Timing and Seasonal Funding

The modest first-quarter NIM decline was largely attributed to the timing of late-2025 Fed moves and predictable seasonal deposit outflows that required more short-term wholesale funding. Executives framed these pressures as temporary and pointed to the stronger March exit margin as evidence that spreads are already starting to normalize as funding dynamics improve.

Deposit Pricing Pressure and Competition

Management acknowledged sustained deposit pricing pressure across the industry as banks compete aggressively for commercial and corporate relationships. F.N.B. reported a cumulative spot deposit beta of roughly 27% since the start of the rate-cut cycle and plans continued targeted efforts to reduce overall funding costs while still defending and growing key client deposits.

Rising Noninterest Expense from Investments

Noninterest expense increased 4.5% year-over-year to $257.9 million, reflecting both growth and elevated investment levels, particularly in technology, facilities and customer programs. Occupancy and equipment costs rose 11%, while other expenses climbed 30% on higher fraud losses, litigation and mortgage down payment support, and management now expects full-year costs to land toward the top of its guidance range.

CRE Payoffs and Concentration Trends

Commercial real estate balances shrank by $110 million sequentially due to anticipated payoffs, highlighting an intentional effort to manage risk in a sensitive portfolio. Even with this decline, CRE exposure remains elevated at around 194% of Tier 1 capital plus allowance, though management expects this metric to continue easing before settling at a more comfortable longer-term level.

Modest Increases in Delinquencies and NPLs

Credit metrics showed slight but manageable deterioration, with delinquencies, nonperforming loans and OREO each up about 3 basis points sequentially. Overall delinquency sits near 74 basis points and NPLs around 34 basis points, while net charge-offs remain low at 18 basis points, prompting management to stay vigilant but not alarmed about emerging credit trends.

Geopolitical Uncertainty and Qualitative Overlays

F.N.B. kept qualitative overlays in its reserve framework tied to ongoing conflict in the Middle East and broader geopolitical and trade-policy risks. While these issues have not yet produced material credit stress in the portfolio, management signaled that they remain key watch factors that could influence future provisioning decisions if conditions deteriorate.

Regulatory and Capital Risk from Proposed Rules

Executives flagged proposed Basel III and related regulatory capital changes as a meaningful long-term variable for the sector, noting that final rules could alter optimal capital structures and deployment. The company has not baked potential impacts into current capital-return plans, leaving room to adjust buybacks or growth strategies if the new framework ultimately proves more demanding.

Guidance and Outlook

Looking ahead, F.N.B. reaffirmed its 2026 outlook for mid-single-digit growth in period-end loans and deposits and guided full-year net interest income to between $1.495 billion and $1.535 billion, with second-quarter NII expected at $370–$380 million. Noninterest income is projected at $370–$390 million, expenses at $1.00–$1.02 billion with strong positive leverage, and provision expense at $85–$105 million, supported by a March exit margin of 3.30% and robust capital and buyback capacity.

F.N.B.’s call painted a picture of a bank leaning into growth and innovation while keeping credit quality and capital strength firmly intact. Earnings momentum, diversified revenue and stable funding underpin a constructive outlook, though investors will be watching deposit costs, expense discipline and regulatory developments as key swing factors in the quarters ahead.

data.title: Regions Financial Corp. Highlights Growth Amid Margin Pressure
data.description:
Regions Financial Corp. ((RF)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Regions Financial Corp.’s latest earnings call struck an upbeat tone, as management emphasized solid profit growth, improving credit quality, disciplined costs, and tangible progress on technology transformation. While acknowledging margin pressure, softer fee income, and a securities loss, executives framed these as manageable headwinds against a backdrop of reaffirmed guidance and ongoing capital returns.

Robust GAAP Earnings and EPS Momentum

Regions reported Q1 net income of $539 million and earnings per share of $0.62, marking an 11% rise in earnings and a 15% EPS increase versus adjusted results a year ago. Management highlighted this as evidence that the franchise is generating healthy profitability even as it navigates a more competitive and rate‑sensitive environment.

Pre-Provision Income and Strong Profitability Metrics

Adjusted pretax, pre‑provision income reached $805 million, up 4% year over year, underlining resilient core earnings power. Return on tangible common equity came in at a robust 18%, positioning Regions among the stronger regional banks in terms of shareholder returns.

Loan Growth Driven by High-Quality Origination

Ending loans grew 2% with average balances up about 1%, supported by a late‑quarter acceleration that produced $2.3 billion in point‑to‑point growth. Roughly 80% of new loans went to existing clients and about two‑thirds of the growth was investment‑grade, with the remainder mostly near investment‑grade, underscoring disciplined risk‑taking.

Deposit Franchise Strength and Lower Funding Costs

Deposits increased roughly 1% with average balances modestly higher, signaling stability in the funding base. Interest‑bearing deposit costs fell 13 basis points to an exit rate of 1.69%, while noninterest‑bearing balances held in the low‑30% range, and management expects average deposits to grow at a low‑single‑digit pace by 2026.

NII Trajectory and Net Interest Margin Outlook

Net interest margin was 3.67%, slightly below internal expectations, but the bank reiterated full‑year net interest income growth of 2.5% to 4%. Management expects NII to rebound about 2% in Q2 and sees NIM exiting 2026 in the low 3.70s, supported by asset mix actions and fixed‑rate asset turnover.

Fee Revenue Strength in Core Businesses

Wealth management fees rose 9% year over year, while treasury management revenue grew 6% from the prior quarter on solid payments activity and capital markets revenue improved 5%. The bank guided full‑year adjusted noninterest income growth of 3% to 5%, signaling confidence in diversified fee engines despite some market‑related pressures.

Expense Discipline and Positive Operating Leverage

Adjusted noninterest expense fell 4% from the prior quarter, reflecting tight cost control and efficiency initiatives. For the full year, Regions expects adjusted expenses to rise 1.5% to 3.5% yet still deliver positive operating leverage as revenue outpaces cost growth.

Improving Credit Trends and Solid Reserve Coverage

Credit metrics continued to improve, with annualized net charge‑offs easing to 54 basis points and the nonperforming loan ratio ticking down to 71 basis points. The allowance for credit losses declined by $39 million to a 1.68% ratio, covering nonperforming loans at 238%, and management maintained full‑year charge‑off guidance of 40 to 50 basis points.

Capital Returns and Regulatory Readiness

Regions ended the quarter with an estimated CET1 ratio of 10.7% and returned substantial capital via $401 million of share repurchases and $227 million in common dividends. A preliminary read of proposed capital rules suggests a fully implemented Basel III CET1 ratio near 10.4%, and management plans to run CET1 around the midpoint of a 9.25% to 9.75% operating range.

Technology Transformation and Talent Investments

The bank reported meaningful progress on its technology overhaul, with a new commercial lending system and small business digital origination platform slated for deployment this summer. Testing of a new core deposit system is underway, with a pilot planned for Q3 and full conversion targeted for 2027, while strategic hiring is largely on track and banker attrition is easing.

Margin Compression from Asset Spread Tightening

Net interest margin was pressured by tighter asset spreads, especially in larger investment‑grade commercial and industrial lending, as competitive pricing and market dynamics squeezed yields. Paydowns of higher‑yielding loans and remixing toward higher‑quality credits also weighed on margin in the near term, even as they improve risk‑adjusted returns longer term.

Soft Noninterest Revenue on Linked-Quarter Comparison

Adjusted noninterest revenue slipped 2% from the prior quarter, reflecting volatility in certain line items rather than broad‑based weakness. Other noninterest income was particularly soft, dropping 29% due mainly to swings in commercial lease sales, which shifted from $6 million of gains last quarter to $7 million of losses.

Seasonal Drag in Card and ATM Fee Income

Card and ATM fees declined 5% sequentially, which management characterized as typical seasonal softness in consumer transaction activity. Consumer service charges were flat year over year, with some one‑time factors dampening growth comparisons and masking underlying stability in the fee base.

Securities Repositioning Loss for Long-Term Benefit

After quarter‑end, Regions sold about $900 million of shorter‑duration securities at a $40 million loss to extend the portfolio’s duration profile. Management framed the move as a strategic repositioning with an expected payback period of around two years, aimed at supporting future margin and earnings.

Ongoing Workouts in Select Risk Portfolios

The bank continues to work through previously flagged portfolios in office, multifamily, transportation, and communications, with a few sizable credits still under active workout. While these legacy exposures continue to influence the 40 to 50 basis points charge‑off outlook, management signaled that remaining risks are concentrated and trending lower over time.

Market Volatility Weighs on Capital Markets Activity

Real estate capital markets have remained subdued for multiple quarters amid higher rates and volatility, limiting deal flow and fee generation. Regions expects its capital markets revenue to sit toward the lower end of the $90 million to $105 million quarterly range in Q2, reflecting continued market headwinds rather than franchise‑specific issues.

Regulatory Reporting Complexity and AOCI Optics

Management noted that including accumulated other comprehensive income in capital ratios under the proposed rule would reduce reported CET1 to about 9.4%. While pro forma adjustments and risk‑weighted asset reductions support underlying capital, executives acknowledged that the transition could create perception challenges they will manage carefully.

Pressure on Lower-Income Consumers

Regions observed pockets of stress among lower‑income customers, even as overall consumer health remains relatively sound. Larger income tax refunds have provided some offset, but management is monitoring this segment closely for early signs of broader credit deterioration.

Forward-Looking Guidance and Strategic Positioning

Looking ahead to 2026, Regions reaffirmed expectations for low‑single‑digit growth in average loans and deposits, net interest income growth of 2.5% to 4%, and NIM exiting in the low 3.70s, helped by about $9 billion of fixed‑rate asset turnover and recent securities repositioning. The bank also guided to 3% to 5% adjusted noninterest income growth, modest expense increases with positive operating leverage, net charge‑offs between 40 and 50 basis points, and CET1 targeted around the midpoint of a 9.25% to 9.75% range.

Regions’ earnings call painted a picture of a regional bank balancing growth, risk management, and shareholder returns in a challenging rate and regulatory landscape. With solid profitability, improving credit, and a clear technology and capital plan, management argued that near‑term margin and fee headwinds are manageable, leaving the long‑term investment case intact for patient shareholders.

data.title: Badger Meter Earnings Call Balances Weak Q1 With Pipeline
data.description:
Badger Meter ((BMI)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Badger Meter’s latest earnings call painted a cautious but composed picture for investors. Management acknowledged a soft first quarter, marked by lower revenue and earnings as project timing and short-cycle weakness weighed on results. Yet they emphasized robust cash generation, resilient margins, and a significantly expanded AMI project pipeline as reasons to stay optimistic about the medium term.

Strong Cash Generation and Capital Return

Badger Meter generated roughly $30 million of free cash flow in the first quarter, keeping pace with last year despite softer sales. The company also returned capital to shareholders by repurchasing 256,000 shares for $38 million and still has $115 million left under its authorization.

Healthy Gross Margins Despite Volume Pressure

Gross margin came in at 41.7%, near the high end of the firm’s 39%–42% target range, even as volumes declined. The modest 120 basis-point year-over-year margin compression underscored the company’s pricing discipline and cost control in a weaker revenue environment.

Expanding AMI Project Funnel Underpins Growth

Management highlighted a significantly larger pipeline of awarded AMI projects expected to begin deployment in 2026. These projects represent an estimated 2.6 million to 3.6 million connections over multiple years, roughly three to four-and-a-half times the size of the previously highlighted 800,000-connection cohort.

PRASA Project Begins to Move Forward

Badger Meter received the first meaningful purchase order for the major PRASA project during the quarter, marking a key milestone. Utility installation partners are poised to begin deployments around midyear, boosting management’s confidence in the ramp trajectory for this flagship engagement.

Strategic Acquisition of UDLive Adds EPS Accretion

The company signed a definitive agreement to buy UDLive for $100 million, funded with cash and contingent consideration. UDLive, which generated about $22 million in trailing twelve-month revenue with positive operating profit, is expected to close in April and be accretive to earnings per share in its first year.

Beyond-the-Meter Offerings Show Bright Spots

Badger Meter’s beyond-the-meter portfolio delivered growth that partly offset weaker metering product sales. The company cited higher revenue from BEACON SaaS, SmartCover, and water quality and network monitoring products as a highlight in an otherwise softer demand backdrop.

Measured Cost Discipline to Protect Margins

To preserve profitability while revenue is pressured, management enacted targeted cost measures, including a 10% salary reduction for executives for six months. They also stressed ongoing discipline in selling, engineering, and administrative spending to help sustain margin integrity through the cycle.

Balance Sheet Strength and Tariff Stability

Primary working capital as a percentage of sales improved to 20.0% at quarter-end from 20.9% at year-end, reinforcing the company’s solid balance sheet posture. Management also reported no material change in tariff exposure, limiting an additional source of volatility for margins.

Revenue Contraction Highlights Near-Term Softness

Total sales fell to $202 million in the quarter, a 9% decline from a year earlier and roughly $15 million to $20 million below internal expectations. Management mainly blamed project pacing and weaker short-cycle orders for the shortfall, framing the downturn as timing-driven rather than structural.

Utility Water Segment Under Pressure

Utility water sales dropped 10% year over year as some projects shifted out and near-term order activity slowed. This segment, a core driver of the business, felt the brunt of both delayed deployments and softer, shorter-cycle purchasing patterns from customers.

Profitability and EPS Hit by Lower Volumes

Operating earnings declined to about $35.2 million with a margin of 17.4%, down from 22.2% in the prior year period. Diluted EPS slid to $0.93 from $1.30, a roughly 28.5% drop, reflecting deleveraging on lower sales despite solid gross margin performance.

SEA Deleverage Amplifies Earnings Pressure

Selling, engineering, and administrative expenses rose to $49.2 million, up $3.1 million year over year. As a share of sales, these expenses expanded by about 360 basis points, driven by volume deleverage and transaction-related costs, including approximately $1.2 million tied to the UDLive deal.

Short-Cycle Order Volatility Adds Uncertainty

The most variable portion of the business—short-cycle orders—was notably weaker in the quarter and represented a major factor behind the revenue miss. Management stressed that timing variability and limited visibility in this bucket introduce near-term uncertainty, even as longer-term project demand remains intact.

Guidance and Forward-Looking Outlook

Looking ahead, management expects 2026 to be heavily back-half weighted and now guides for full-year organic revenue, excluding UDLive, to be flat with 2025. Second-quarter results should improve sequentially from the first-quarter trough but remain down year over year against last year’s record quarter, with gross margins still targeted within the 39%–42% range.

Project Timing and Margin Mix Risks

While the company’s pipeline of awarded projects is sizeable, management acknowledged execution and timing risks linked to labor, weather, regulatory processes, and funding cycles. They also noted that large turnkey engagements like PRASA may carry different margin profiles that can pressure blended gross margins even as they help leverage overhead.

In sum, Badger Meter’s earnings call balanced clear near-term softness with a constructive medium-term setup anchored by a hefty AMI backlog, the PRASA rollout, and the UDLive acquisition. Investors will be watching closely to see whether the back-half recovery and project ramps arrive as planned, and whether cost discipline and strong cash generation can continue to offset short-cycle volatility.

data.title: Seri Industrial Boosts Adjusted Earnings as It Ramps Up European Battery Investments
data.description:
Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

An update from Seri Industrial S.p.A. ( (IT:SERI) ) is now available.

Seri Industrial reported adjusted 2025 revenues of about €372 million and an adjusted EBITDA of €65.2 million, with profitability boosted by strong growth in the Sustainable Mobility and Batteries divisions. Although headline profit fell year on year due to prior one-off gains, adjusted net income swung sharply higher, supported by a 7% increase in headcount and continued investment.

The group’s net financial debt rose significantly to fund some €88.8 million of capex, largely tied to the Teverola 2 IPCEI-backed battery project, while the fully operational Teverola 1 plant is being used both for commercial production and process optimisation. By securing a €150 million revolving credit line and advancing contracts with leading European suppliers, Seri is accelerating its European battery manufacturing footprint, which could strengthen its competitive stance in sustainable mobility supply chains despite the near-term leverage increase.

The most recent analyst rating on (IT:SERI) stock is a Hold with a EUR4.20 price target. To see the full list of analyst forecasts on Seri Industrial S.p.A. stock, see the IT:SERI Stock Forecast page.

More about Seri Industrial S.p.A.

Seri Industrial S.p.A. operates in the industrial and sustainable mobility sectors, with activities spanning batteries, plastics, and related technologies. The group focuses on European-made battery cells and systems, leveraging plants such as Teverola 1 to supply technologically advanced, higher-margin products while positioning itself against lower-priced Asian competitors.

On April 17, 2026, Royal Bank of Canada issued Senior Global Medium-Term Notes, Series J, in two tranches totaling US$3.0 billion under its U.S. shelf registration, consisting of US$1.5 billion of 4.400% senior fixed-to-floating rate notes due April 17, 2030 and US$1.5 billion of 4.612% senior fixed-to-floating rate notes due May 3, 2032. The latest announcement is out from Faraday Future Intelligent Electric ( (FFAI) ).

On April 14, 2026, Faraday Future director and Global Co-Chief Executive Officer Matthias Aydt resigned from the board effective immediately for personal reasons and indicated he would also step down from his executive role at a time determined by the board. On April 16, 2026, director Jie (Jay) Sheng resigned from the board, while on the same date executive director Chui Tin Mok left the board to focus on leading the company’s business execution in the United Arab Emirates and the broader Middle East, though he remains an executive officer and Head of FF Middle East.

Also on April 16, 2026, shareholder FF Top Holding LLC nominated human resources head Xiao (Lucky) Jiang and independent director Kevin Chen to the board, and the board appointed them along with Global President Jiawei (Jerry) Wang as new directors. The appointments, which include new committee assignments for Wang and Chen, consolidate FF Top’s board influence and inject capital markets, human resources, and macroeconomic expertise into Faraday Future’s governance as it navigates leadership changes and regional expansion.

Bioxcel Therapeutics ( (BTAI) ) has provided an announcement.

On March 27, 2026, BioXcel Therapeutics, Inc. entered into a Ninth Amendment to its April 19, 2022 Credit Agreement and Guaranty, under which it agreed to provide additional equity-linked consideration to its lenders. On April 15, 2026, the company issued these lenders warrants to purchase up to 1,353,729 common shares at an exercise price of $0.01 per share, expiring seven years from issuance, signaling further alignment of lender interests with the company’s equity.

Also on April 15, 2026, BioXcel executed a Fourth Amended and Restated Registration Rights Agreement with the same lenders to register the common shares issuable upon exercise of the new warrants. The warrants and any underlying shares are being issued under a private placement exemption from Securities Act registration, with the lenders representing investment intent, reflecting a financing structure that may affect future shareholder dilution and capital flexibility.

data.title: CXApp Raises Capital Through Private Equity Share Issuance
data.description:
Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

CXApp ( (CXAI) ) has issued an update.

CXApp Inc. issued a total of 7,304,178 shares of common stock to Avondale Capital, LLC under two pre-paid purchase arrangements tied to an earlier securities purchase agreement dated March 26, 2025. The stock, priced between approximately $0.1449 and $0.1544 per share, was issued in private, non-public offerings on April 13, 16 and 17, 2026, indicating the company’s continued reliance on structured equity financing to raise capital outside public markets, with potential dilution implications for existing shareholders.

The most recent analyst rating on (CXAI) stock is a Buy with a $2.00 price target. To see the full list of analyst forecasts on CXApp stock, see the CXAI Stock Forecast page.

data.title: Wellgistics Forms AI-Driven Pharmacy Access Joint Venture
data.description:
Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

An announcement from Wellgistics Health Inc ( (WGRX) ) is now available.

On April 13 and 14, 2026, Wellgistics Health, Inc. entered into a collaboration and definitive joint venture agreement with Kare PharmTech’s subsidiary Kare Rx Hub, creating a new limited liability company called Healthstar in which Wellgistics will hold a 51% stake and Kare Hub 49%. The deal includes Wellgistics acquiring certain Kare Hub intellectual property and related assets for up to $2 million paid in unregistered common stock, subject to due diligence, lock-up provisions, and customary closing conditions, and is expected to rely on a private offering exemption.

The joint venture combines Wellgistics’ EinsteinRx and HubRx AI platforms with KareRx’s AI-driven digital hub to form an integrated ecosystem that streamlines the prescription journey from intake through fulfillment and supports functions such as eligibility checks, benefits verification, prior authorization workflows, routing, and direct-to-patient delivery. By aligning clinical, operational, and commercial teams across both organizations and expanding connectivity among independent pharmacies, provider networks, and telehealth channels, the platform aims to accelerate pharmaceutical access, enhance commercialization efficiency, strengthen direct-to-patient and decentralized care models, and potentially reach more than 200,000 patient lives, though the timing and extent of full operational integration remain uncertain.

More about Wellgistics Health Inc

Wellgistics Health, Inc. is a health information technology and pharmaceutical distribution company that integrates its EinsteinRx AI dispensing optimization platform with a blockchain-enabled smart contracts system called PharmacyChain to improve the prescription drug dispensing journey. The company connects more than 6,500 pharmacies and over 200 manufacturers, offering wholesale distribution, digital prescription routing, direct-to-patient delivery, and AI-powered hub services focused on eligibility, adherence, onboarding, prior authorization, and cash-pay fulfillment for independent pharmacies in the U.S. prescription drug market.

Kare Rx Hub, owned by Kare PharmTech, LLC, operates an AI-based digital hub that serves retail pharmacies, telemedicine, and specialty pharmaceutical “lite” products, with hundreds of physician-provider and independent pharmacy relationships. Kare Rx Hub’s platform is designed to connect providers, pharmacies, and patients through technology-driven solutions that support access to therapies across participating networks.

Average Trading Volume: 12,140,306

Technical Sentiment Signal: Strong Sell

Current Market Cap: $17.62M
